eli3: Chunking is the process of splitting large documents into smaller pieces before storing them in a vector database. You can't embed an entire 100-page PDF as one unit — the embedding would be too general to be useful for specific questions. Chunks are the units of retrieval.

Chunk size matters a lot. Too small (50 tokens): each chunk is a sentence, highly precise but missing context when retrieved. Too large (2000 tokens): each chunk is several paragraphs, lots of context but retrieval is imprecise (many irrelevant sentences dilute the signal). Most systems use 256–512 token chunks with some overlap between adjacent chunks.

Modern chunking strategies go beyond simple fixed-size splitting: semantic chunking splits at topic boundaries, parent-child chunking stores small and large versions of the same content, and late chunking uses modern embedding models that chunk and embed in a single pass.

whenWhy.title: Embedding quality degrades with long texts
whenWhy.body: Embedding models (text-embedding-3-small, BGE) are trained on short passages (typically < 512 tokens). When you embed longer texts, the model averages information across all tokens — the embedding becomes a blurry summary that doesn't represent any specific detail well. Chunking at ~512 tokens aligns with the embedding model's training distribution, producing embeddings that capture specific facts and concepts. Retrieval precision is directly proportional to how well the chunk embedding captures what a relevant query would match.

whenWhy.title: Different queries need different granularity
whenWhy.body: A question about a specific number ('What was Apple's 2023 revenue?') is best answered by a small, precise chunk containing that sentence. A question requiring context ('Why did Apple's revenue decline in 2023?') needs a larger chunk with surrounding explanation. Advanced strategies address this: store both small chunks (for retrieval) and their parent paragraphs (for generation). Retrieve small chunks, return parent context. This 'small-to-big' strategy gives retrieval precision and generation context.

whenWhy.title: Boundary effects cause retrieval failures
whenWhy.body: Naive fixed-size chunking splits sentences in the middle, separates a claim from its evidence, or cuts a table row from its header. These boundary errors cause the retrieved chunk to be incomplete or misleading. Chunk overlap (50-100 token overlap between adjacent chunks) partially mitigates this — a sentence cut at a boundary appears in both adjacent chunks. Semantic chunking (split at paragraph/section boundaries, not at fixed token counts) eliminates most boundary issues but is more complex to implement.

deepDiveIntro: Chunking strategy is arguably the most impactful and under-appreciated decision in RAG pipeline design. Naive fixed-size chunking is fast but degrades retrieval quality significantly compared to content-aware strategies. The RAGAS (Es et al. 2023) evaluation framework has been used to empirically compare chunking strategies; sentence-window and parent-child chunking consistently outperform fixed-size chunking on context precision and recall metrics.

LlamaIndex and LangChain both provide multiple chunking implementations: RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter (LangChain default), SentenceSplitter (LlamaIndex default), SemanticChunker (split on embedding similarity drops), and custom hierarchical splitters. The 'right' strategy depends on document structure and query types — always evaluate on a representative query set before committing.

deepDive.heading: Fixed-Size vs Semantic Chunking
deepDive.body: Fixed-size: split every N tokens (or characters) with overlap M. Simple, fast, no ML required. Default: 512 tokens, 50-token overlap. Weakness: splits mid-sentence, mid-table, mid-list. Best for: homogeneous, well-structured documents (technical manuals, encyclopedia articles). Semantic: use an embedding model to detect topic shifts — split when the cosine similarity between adjacent sentence embeddings drops below a threshold. Produces chunks that are semantically coherent but variable in length. Slower (requires embedding every sentence during indexing). Best for: heterogeneous documents with multiple topics (news articles, research papers). LangChain SemanticChunker implements this with three breakpoint threshold types: percentile, standard_deviation, interquartile. [1]

deepDive.heading: Parent-Child and Sentence-Window Chunking
deepDive.body: Parent-child (hierarchical) chunking: index two sizes simultaneously. Child chunks: 128 tokens (high precision for retrieval). Parent chunks: 512 tokens (sufficient context for synthesis). At retrieval time: search child chunk embeddings (small = precise match). At synthesis time: return the parent chunk (large = full context). Implementation: store parent_id in child chunk metadata; after retrieving child chunks, fetch their parents. LlamaIndex AutoMergingRetriever does this automatically. Sentence-window: embed individual sentences; at retrieval, return the sentence ± K surrounding sentences (window_size=3 means return 7 sentences total). More fine-grained than parent-child. [1]

deepDive.heading: Late Chunking with Long-Context Embedding Models
deepDive.body: Late chunking (Jina AI, 2024): a novel approach that avoids the traditional chunking → embedding pipeline. Feed the entire document to a long-context embedding model (jina-embeddings-v2, max 8192 tokens). The model produces one embedding per token (contextual token embeddings). Then pool token embeddings into chunk embeddings by mean-pooling the token embeddings within each chunk boundary. Result: each chunk embedding is contextualised by the full document, not just the chunk itself. This preserves cross-chunk context (e.g., a pronoun in chunk 3 that refers to a noun in chunk 1 is correctly contextualised). Demonstrated to outperform standard chunking on multi-hop retrieval benchmarks. [2]

takeaways: Chunk size trade-off: small = precise retrieval, less context; large = more context, imprecise retrieval. Default: 512 tokens. [1]
Chunk overlap (50-100 tokens) mitigates boundary splits — critical for sentence-spanning facts. [1]
Parent-child: embed small child chunks (retrieve precisely), return large parent chunks (synthesise with context). [1]
Semantic chunking: split on embedding similarity drops — coherent topics, variable length, slower indexing. [1]
Late chunking: contextualised chunk embeddings from long-context models — better cross-chunk retrieval. [2]

edgeCases.q: How do you chunk structured data like tables and code files?
edgeCases.a: Tables: never split a table across chunks — extract each table as a complete unit (use pdfplumber or Camelot for PDFs). Embed the table as Markdown (preserves structure). If a table is very large (> 1000 tokens), split by row groups with the header row repeated in each chunk. Alternative: convert the table to natural language ('Row 1: Q1 2024 revenue was $5.2B, representing 12% YoY growth'). Code: split at function/class boundaries, not at character count. Use AST parsing (Python's ast module, tree-sitter for multi-language) to identify function definitions; each function is one chunk. Never split inside a function. Add metadata: {file_path, function_name, class_name, language}. For code RAG, code-specific embedding models (CodeBERT, code-embedding-3-small) outperform text embedding models.

edgeCases.q: How do you determine the optimal chunk size experimentally?
edgeCases.a: Systematic evaluation: (1) Create a golden dataset: 50-100 question-answer pairs over your documents where you know which passage contains the answer; (2) Test chunk sizes: 128, 256, 512, 1024 tokens; for each size, build the index and run all questions; (3) Metrics: retrieval hit rate (does the correct chunk appear in top-k retrieved results?), MRR (mean reciprocal rank of the correct chunk), and end-to-end answer accuracy; (4) Pick the chunk size that maximises the combination of hit rate and end-to-end accuracy. Typical finding: 256-512 is optimal for most document types; very long-form documents (textbooks) benefit from 512-1024. The evaluation cost is low (a few hundred LLM calls) but prevents weeks of debugging production retrieval failures.
